paper_title: Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns
original_problem: Mechanistic interpretability for transformers requires expensive training (SAEs need GPU-hours, probes need labeled datasets) to discover feature directions, making it hard to scale to large models.

reconstructed_problem.root_cause: The field assumed features live in rotated subspaces (superposition hypothesis), requiring learned rotations to extract them. In reality, individual dimensions already encode discrete semantic content via their sign, making training-free readout possible.
reconstructed_problem.mechanism: Each hidden dimension acts as an independent binary register: sign(+1/-1)=semantic content, magnitude=confidence. Features are coalitions of dimensions with consistent sign patterns, readable via simple sign-majority voting.
reconstructed_problem.why_existing_solutions_fail: SAEs search for sparse features in a rotated basis, but the standard basis is already sparse (100% feature yield, 99% sparsity with simple unsupervised seeds). Probes converge to axis-aligned weights anyway.

key_insights.insight: Transformer dimensions are nearly independent (MI < 0.006 bits) — no superposition binding in real models
key_insights.implication: No rotation needed; the standard basis IS the feature basis

key_insights.insight: Sign carries semantic content, magnitude carries confidence
key_insights.implication: Binary feature detection via sign voting; continuous via magnitude weighting

key_insights.insight: Structure appears across modalities (language, vision, audio)
key_insights.implication: Likely a convergent property of gradient descent on transformers, not language-specific

components.operation: One forward pass per token, store hidden state
Per-dim AUC measures how well sign separates category from full vocab
components.output: Registered dims + sign prototype per category

components.operation: Count fraction of registered dims where sign matches expected polarity

what_problem_remains: Tested only on 4-32B language models and 86M vision/audio models; unclear at 70B+
Depth-gated causality: sign-flip only works near output, earlier layers re-derive
Directional asymmetry: can suppress (flip away) but not induce concepts (flip toward)
Polysemy sensing at Qwen3-32B is near-chance, attributed to low D/V ratio
